quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 5 Quiz 6
quiz.subtitle: Triangle Inequality Theorem and Side-Angle Relationships
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: The Triangle Inequality Theorem states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be:
options: Greater than the length of the third side
Equal to the length of the third side
Less than the length of the third side
Greater than or equal to the length of the third side
questions.explanation: The Triangle Inequality Theorem states that for any triangle, the sum of the lengths of any two sides is strictly greater than the length of the remaining side.

questions.question: Which set of side lengths can form a triangle?
questions.explanation: Check the Triangle Inequality: $6 + 7 = 13 > 11$, $6 + 11 = 17 > 7$, $7 + 11 = 18 > 6$. All three inequalities hold, so these sides form a triangle.

questions.question: Which set of side lengths cannot form a triangle?
questions.explanation: For $3$, $4$, $8$: check $3 + 4 = 7$, which is less than $8$. The Triangle Inequality fails, so these sides cannot form a triangle.

questions.question: A triangle has two sides of lengths $5$ cm and $9$ cm. Which could be the length of the third side?
questions.explanation: By the Triangle Inequality, the third side $x$ must satisfy: $5 + 9 > x$, so $x < 14$; and $5 + x > 9$, so $x > 4$. The only option between $4$ and $14$ is $7$ cm.

questions.question: In a triangle, the largest angle is always opposite which side?
questions.explanation: The largest angle of a triangle is always opposite the longest side. Similarly, the smallest angle is opposite the shortest side.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$, $AB = 8$, $BC = 5$, and $AC = 10$. Which angle is the largest?
questions.explanation: The longest side is $AC = 10$, so the angle opposite it, $\angle B$, is the largest angle.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$, $m\angle P = 85^\circ$, $m\angle Q = 45^\circ$, and $m\angle R = 50^\circ$. Which side is the longest?
questions.explanation: The longest side is opposite the largest angle. $\angle P = 85^\circ$ is the largest, so $\overline{QR}$ (opposite $\angle P$) is the longest side.

questions.question: In $\triangle XYZ$, $m\angle X = 30^\circ$ and $m\angle Y = 110^\circ$. Which statement must be true?
questions.explanation: First find $m\angle Z = 180^\circ - 30^\circ - 110^\circ = 40^\circ$. The largest angle is $\angle Y = 110^\circ$, so the side opposite it, $\overline{XZ}$, is the longest. So $XZ > XY$ and $XZ > YZ$.

questions.question: Can a triangle have sides of lengths $4$, $4$, and $8$?
options: No, because $4 + 4 = 8$, and the sum must be strictly greater than the third side
Yes, because all three specified side lengths possess a positive value
Yes, because two matching side lengths automatically satisfy the triangle condition
No, because the given side metrics must all be entirely different lengths
questions.explanation: The Triangle Inequality requires the sum of any two sides to be strictly greater than the third. Here, $4 + 4 = 8$, not greater. This would form a degenerate triangle (three collinear points).

questions.question: In $\triangle DEF$, $DE = 7$, $EF = 9$, and $DF = 6$. Which list orders the angles from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: Order sides from shortest to longest: $DF = 6$ (opposite $\angle E$), $DE = 7$ (opposite $\angle F$), $EF = 9$ (opposite $\angle D$). So angles from smallest to largest: $\angle E$, $\angle F$, $\angle D$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Triangle Inequality Theorem and the side-angle relationships within a single triangle.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the basics but review that the sum of two sides must be strictly greater than the third, and the largest angle is always opposite the longest side.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: the Triangle Inequality requires $a + b > c$ for all three combinations; the longest side faces the largest angle, and the shortest side faces the smallest angle.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! To check if three sides form a triangle, verify all three inequalities. In any triangle, sides and their opposite angles follow the same order from smallest to largest.
